title: Massive Haemoptysis
oneLiner: Life-threatening airway emergency; usually >300mL/24h; causes asphyxiation, hypovolaemia, or both; requires immediate airway control and source control.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway obstruction / asphyxiation** – patient unable to clear blood from trachea; *single finding:* stridor, gurgling, or silent chest with respiratory distress.
- **Hypovolaemic shock** – rapid exsanguination; *single finding:* systolic BP <90mmHg or haemoglobin drop >2g/dL within 1 hour.
- **Mimic: Haematemesis from oesophageal varices** – coffee-ground vomitus, no frothy blood, pH acidic, often history of liver disease; *differentiator:* ask to cough specimen – haemoptysis is bright red, frothy, alkaline.
- **Mimic: Epistaxis with posterior drainage** – patient may spit blood; *differentiator:* ask to tilt head forward – if bleeding from nose, blood will drip from nostrils.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Coughing up bright red, frothy blood (volume >300mL in 24h or any single episode causing distress)
  - Gurgling sensation in throat, dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain (if underlying infection or infarction)
  - Lightheadedness, palpitations, presyncope (signs of hypovolaemia)
- Key exam findings:
  - Vital signs: tachycardia, hypotension, tachypnoea, desaturation
  - Respiratory: reduced air entry on side of bleeding, bronchial breathing, crepitations
  - Cardiovascular: signs of shock (cool peripheries, delayed capillary refill)
  - Pallor, anxiety, inability to speak in full sentences
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Bronchiectasis (chronic cough, purulent sputum, clubbing) – usually known diagnosis.  
- **Second:** Lung cancer (haemoptysis + weight loss, smoking history, clubbing) – often recurrent, small volume but massive possible.  
- **Third:** Tuberculosis (haemoptysis + night sweats, fever, apical infiltrates on CXR) – especially in at-risk populations.  
- **Less likely but high stakes:** Aspergilloma (cavitary lesion on CXR, previous TB), pulmonary arteriovenous malformation (PAVM) (associated with Osler-Weber-Rendu, Platypnoea-orthodeoxia), pulmonary embolism with infarction (pleuritic pain, DVT risk, Wells criteria).  
- **Splitter:** Does the patient have known structural lung disease (bronchiectasis, TB cavity) ± sudden change in sputum? If yes, infection/bleeding from same site. If no, consider malignancy or vascular anomaly.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Airway assessment (patency, ability to cough, oxygen saturations, respiratory rate)
  - Check for clotting (INR/PTT if available)
  - Position patient: bleeding side down (lateral decubitus)
  - Secure IV access (2 large-bore cannulae)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb, platelets), U&E, coagulation screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen)
  - Cross-match (group + screen, request 4–6 units if unstable)
  - d-Dimer (if PE suspected – but can be false positive in haemoptysis)
  - Baseline blood gas (pO2, pCO2, pH, lactate)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR (AP or PA) – look for air-space opacification, cavity, mass, effusion, signs of TB or bronchiectasis.
  - **Second-line:** CT thorax with IV contrast (CT pulmonary angiogram if PE suspected, else high-resolution CT for bronchiectasis or mass) – **urgent once stable**.
- **Special tests:**
  - Bronchoscopy (rigid or flexible) – therapeutic and diagnostic; can localise bleeding, apply cold saline/vasopressin, or insert balloon tamponade.
  - Bronchial artery angiography + embolisation (BAE) – if CT shows systemic arterial source.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No single validated score; clinical judgement based on volume (e.g., >300mL/24h or >100mL per event + haemodynamic instability).
- **Never-miss test:** **CT chest with IV contrast** (or urgent bronchoscopy if patient unstable) – omitting this delays identification of resectable cancer or embolisable bleeding source.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  1. **Airway priority:** If patient cannot clear blood (GCS <15, stridor, desaturation despite high-flow O2) – call anaesthetics for **rapid sequence intubation** with large-bore ETT (size 8+). Position bleeding side DOWN if lateralising.
  2. **Resuscitation:** IV crystalloid (500mL bolus, repeat if hypotensive). Transfuse cross-matched blood if available, O-negative if unstable, platelets if <50, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5, fresh frozen plasma if prolonged INR.
  3. **Reverse anticoagulation:** If on warfarin → vitamin K 10mg IV slowly + PCC [check local policy]; if on DOAC → idarucizumab 5g IV (dabigatran) or andexanet alfa [check local policy] (apixaban/rivaretaban); if on heparin → protamine sulphate 1mg per 100 units heparin given.
  4. **Consider tranexamic acid:** 1g IV over 10min (then 1g IV over 8h) – if bleeding persists, uncertain benefit but low risk.
  5. **Bronchoscopy / interventional radiology:** Contact respiratory team and interventional radiology early. Rigid bronchoscopy for tamponade; bronchial artery embolisation for proximal/massive bleed.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit to HDU/ICU if significant bleeding or requiring respiratory support.
  - Maintain bed rest, head-up 30°, O2 target 94-98%.
  - Repeat Hb, coagulation panel every 6h until stable.
  - Plan definitive therapy: BAE, surgery (lobectomy/segmentectomy), or treatment of underlying cause (antibiotics for infection, anti-TB therapy, radiotherapy for tumour).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Treat underlying disease: bronchiectasis (airway clearance, antibiotics), TB (RIPE therapy), lung cancer (MDT discussion).
  - Consider bronchial artery embolisation for recurrent bleeds (success rate 70-90%).
  - Smoking cessation, referral to chest physician for follow-up.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 80-100mL/h (avoid hypervolaemia if right heart failure)
  - VTE prophylaxis: early chemical prophylaxis (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) **only once bleeding stopped** (usually after 24-48h of no haemoptysis)
  - Diet: NBM initially (risk of aspiration); clear fluids if stable and safe swallow
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 15min until stable, then hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents – reversal of anticoagulation:**
  - Warfarin: Vitamin K 10mg IV slow (over 30min) + prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) [check local policy] (e.g., Beriplex/Balfactar 25-50 units/kg)
  - Dabigatran: Idarucizumab 5g IV
  - Apixaban/Rivaretaban: Andexanet alfa [check local policy] (bolus + infusion)
- **Supportive:**
  - Tranexamic acid: 1g IV over 10min, then 1g IV over 8h
  - Blood products: cross-matched PRBC, platelets (if <50x10^9/L), cryoprecipitate (fibrinogen <1.5g/L)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Tranexamic acid: avoid history of thromboembolic disease (unless life-threatening haemoptysis)
  - Protamine: can cause hypotension, bradycardia – give slowly over 10min
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Tranexamic acid: reduce dose in renal impairment (CrCl <30mL/min: 500mg IV q12h)
  - Vitamin K is safe in hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Overtransfusion of crystalloid** causing dilutional coagulopathy and increased bleeding; use balanced resuscitation with blood products early.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Asphyxiation (most feared) – need urgent intubation
  - Hypovolaemic shock – >30% blood loss
  - Myocardial ischaemia or stroke secondary to hypotension
  - Rebleeding (up to 30% after BAE)
- **Chronic:**
  - Fibrosis/stenosis from bronchial artery embolisation
  - Recurrence of underlying disease (bronchiectasis, TB, tumour)
  - Anaemia
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality in massive haemoptysis is 10-50% without intervention; prompt identification and localising treatment reduces it to <10%. Recovery depends on cause – bronchiectasis has good prognosis with embolisation; malignancy has poor (5-year <20%).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Position bleeding side down** (lateral decubitus) – prevents spillover into contralateral lung; mark the side on the bed.
- **Request rigid bronchoscopy early** – it can prevent asphyxiation and allow balloon tamponade.
- **Check coagulation panel in every patient** – even if not on anticoagulants; liver disease, DIC, or heparin use can cause coagulopathy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t delay intubation** waiting for bronchoscopy in a deteriorating patient – a cuffed ETT alone can protect the airway.
- **Don’t give excess crystalloid** without blood products – worsens bleeding by diluting clotting factors.
- **Don’t forget to reverse anticoagulation** – failing to get prothrombin complex concentrate quickly can be fatal.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Any recurrence of haemoptysis (even small volume), new shortness of breath, pleuritic chest pain, or feeling faint/passing out.
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic within 2 weeks; if due to malignancy, urgent oncology referral.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- Call senior (registrar/consultant) and anaesthetics if: NEWS2 ≥5, SpO2 <90% on 15L O2, systolic BP <90mmHg despite fluids, lactate >2.5mmol/L, GCS <15, or any episode of massive haemoptysis (>100mL).
- Call ICU/outreach if: intubated for airway protection, on vasopressors, or undergoing bronchial artery embolisation.
- Crash call if: cardiac arrest, loss of airway, or massive haemoptysis >500mL in one episode.

rawMarkdown:
## Massive Haemoptysis
**One-liner:** Life-threatening airway emergency; usually >300mL/24h; causes asphyxiation, hypovolaemia, or both; requires immediate airway control and source control.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway obstruction / asphyxiation** – patient unable to clear blood from trachea; *single finding:* stridor, gurgling, or silent chest with respiratory distress.
- **Hypovolaemic shock** – rapid exsanguination; *single finding:* systolic BP <90mmHg or haemoglobin drop >2g/dL within 1 hour.
- **Mimic: Haematemesis from oesophageal varices** – coffee-ground vomitus, no frothy blood, pH acidic, often history of liver disease; *differentiator:* ask to cough specimen – haemoptysis is bright red, frothy, alkaline.
- **Mimic: Epistaxis with posterior drainage** – patient may spit blood; *differentiator:* ask to tilt head forward – if bleeding from nose, blood will drip from nostrils.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Coughing up bright red, frothy blood (volume >300mL in 24h or any single episode causing distress)
  - Gurgling sensation in throat, dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain (if underlying infection or infarction)
  - Lightheadedness, palpitations, presyncope (signs of hypovolaemia)
- Key exam findings:
  - Vital signs: tachycardia, hypotension, tachypnoea, desaturation
  - Respiratory: reduced air entry on side of bleeding, bronchial breathing, crepitations
  - Cardiovascular: signs of shock (cool peripheries, delayed capillary refill)
  - Pallor, anxiety, inability to speak in full sentences

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Bronchiectasis (chronic cough, purulent sputum, clubbing) – usually known diagnosis.  
- **Second:** Lung cancer (haemoptysis + weight loss, smoking history, clubbing) – often recurrent, small volume but massive possible.  
- **Third:** Tuberculosis (haemoptysis + night sweats, fever, apical infiltrates on CXR) – especially in at-risk populations.  
- **Less likely but high stakes:** Aspergilloma (cavitary lesion on CXR, previous TB), pulmonary arteriovenous malformation (PAVM) (associated with Osler-Weber-Rendu, Platypnoea-orthodeoxia), pulmonary embolism with infarction (pleuritic pain, DVT risk, Wells criteria).  
- **Splitter:** Does the patient have known structural lung disease (bronchiectasis, TB cavity) ± sudden change in sputum? If yes, infection/bleeding from same site. If no, consider malignancy or vascular anomaly.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Airway assessment (patency, ability to cough, oxygen saturations, respiratory rate)
  - Check for clotting (INR/PTT if available)
  - Position patient: bleeding side down (lateral decubitus)
  - Secure IV access (2 large-bore cannulae)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (Hb, platelets), U&E, coagulation screen (PT, APTT, fibrinogen)
  - Cross-match (group + screen, request 4–6 units if unstable)
  - d-Dimer (if PE suspected – but can be false positive in haemoptysis)
  - Baseline blood gas (pO2, pCO2, pH, lactate)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR (AP or PA) – look for air-space opacification, cavity, mass, effusion, signs of TB or bronchiectasis.
  - **Second-line:** CT thorax with IV contrast (CT pulmonary angiogram if PE suspected, else high-resolution CT for bronchiectasis or mass) – **urgent once stable**.
- **Special tests:**
  - Bronchoscopy (rigid or flexible) – therapeutic and diagnostic; can localise bleeding, apply cold saline/vasopressin, or insert balloon tamponade.
  - Bronchial artery angiography + embolisation (BAE) – if CT shows systemic arterial source.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No single validated score; clinical judgement based on volume (e.g., >300mL/24h or >100mL per event + haemodynamic instability).
- **Never-miss test:** **CT chest with IV contrast** (or urgent bronchoscopy if patient unstable) – omitting this delays identification of resectable cancer or embolisable bleeding source.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  1. **Airway priority:** If patient cannot clear blood (GCS <15, stridor, desaturation despite high-flow O2) – call anaesthetics for **rapid sequence intubation** with large-bore ETT (size 8+). Position bleeding side DOWN if lateralising.
  2. **Resuscitation:** IV crystalloid (500mL bolus, repeat if hypotensive). Transfuse cross-matched blood if available, O-negative if unstable, platelets if <50, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5, fresh frozen plasma if prolonged INR.
  3. **Reverse anticoagulation:** If on warfarin → vitamin K 10mg IV slowly + PCC [check local policy]; if on DOAC → idarucizumab 5g IV (dabigatran) or andexanet alfa [check local policy] (apixaban/rivaretaban); if on heparin → protamine sulphate 1mg per 100 units heparin given.
  4. **Consider tranexamic acid:** 1g IV over 10min (then 1g IV over 8h) – if bleeding persists, uncertain benefit but low risk.
  5. **Bronchoscopy / interventional radiology:** Contact respiratory team and interventional radiology early. Rigid bronchoscopy for tamponade; bronchial artery embolisation for proximal/massive bleed.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit to HDU/ICU if significant bleeding or requiring respiratory support.
  - Maintain bed rest, head-up 30°, O2 target 94-98%.
  - Repeat Hb, coagulation panel every 6h until stable.
  - Plan definitive therapy: BAE, surgery (lobectomy/segmentectomy), or treatment of underlying cause (antibiotics for infection, anti-TB therapy, radiotherapy for tumour).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Treat underlying disease: bronchiectasis (airway clearance, antibiotics), TB (RIPE therapy), lung cancer (MDT discussion).
  - Consider bronchial artery embolisation for recurrent bleeds (success rate 70-90%).
  - Smoking cessation, referral to chest physician for follow-up.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 80-100mL/h (avoid hypervolaemia if right heart failure)
  - VTE prophylaxis: early chemical prophylaxis (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) **only once bleeding stopped** (usually after 24-48h of no haemoptysis)
  - Diet: NBM initially (risk of aspiration); clear fluids if stable and safe swallow
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 15min until stable, then hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents – reversal of anticoagulation:**
  - Warfarin: Vitamin K 10mg IV slow (over 30min) + prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) [check local policy] (e.g., Beriplex/Balfactar 25-50 units/kg)
  - Dabigatran: Idarucizumab 5g IV
  - Apixaban/Rivaretaban: Andexanet alfa [check local policy] (bolus + infusion)
- **Supportive:**
  - Tranexamic acid: 1g IV over 10min, then 1g IV over 8h
  - Blood products: cross-matched PRBC, platelets (if <50x10^9/L), cryoprecipitate (fibrinogen <1.5g/L)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Tranexamic acid: avoid history of thromboembolic disease (unless life-threatening haemoptysis)
  - Protamine: can cause hypotension, bradycardia – give slowly over 10min
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Tranexamic acid: reduce dose in renal impairment (CrCl <30mL/min: 500mg IV q12h)
  - Vitamin K is safe in hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Overtransfusion of crystalloid** causing dilutional coagulopathy and increased bleeding; use balanced resuscitation with blood products early.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Asphyxiation (most feared) – need urgent intubation
  - Hypovolaemic shock – >30% blood loss
  - Myocardial ischaemia or stroke secondary to hypotension
  - Rebleeding (up to 30% after BAE)
- **Chronic:**
  - Fibrosis/stenosis from bronchial artery embolisation
  - Recurrence of underlying disease (bronchiectasis, TB, tumour)
  - Anaemia

### Prognosis
- Mortality in massive haemoptysis is 10-50% without intervention; prompt identification and localising treatment reduces it to <10%. Recovery depends on cause – bronchiectasis has good prognosis with embolisation; malignancy has poor (5-year <20%).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Position bleeding side down** (lateral decubitus) – prevents spillover into contralateral lung; mark the side on the bed.
- **Request rigid bronchoscopy early** – it can prevent asphyxiation and allow balloon tamponade.
- **Check coagulation panel in every patient** – even if not on anticoagulants; liver disease, DIC, or heparin use can cause coagulopathy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t delay intubation** waiting for bronchoscopy in a deteriorating patient – a cuffed ETT alone can protect the airway.
- **Don’t give excess crystalloid** without blood products – worsens bleeding by diluting clotting factors.
- **Don’t forget to reverse anticoagulation** – failing to get prothrombin complex concentrate quickly can be fatal.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Any recurrence of haemoptysis (even small volume), new shortness of breath, pleuritic chest pain, or feeling faint/passing out.
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic within 2 weeks; if due to malignancy, urgent oncology referral.

### Escalation Criteria
- Call senior (registrar/consultant) and anaesthetics if: NEWS2 ≥5, SpO2 <90% on 15L O2, systolic BP <90mmHg despite fluids, lactate >2.5mmol/L, GCS <15, or any episode of massive haemoptysis (>100mL).
- Call ICU/outreach if: intubated for airway protection, on vasopressors, or undergoing bronchial artery embolisation.
- Crash call if: cardiac arrest, loss of airway, or massive haemoptysis >500mL in one episode.
